title: Peace of Westphalia signed
summary: On 24 October 1648, in the twin Westphalian cities of Münster and Osnabrück, more than a hundred delegations of the Holy Roman Empire, France, Sweden, Spain, the Dutch Republic, and the German princes signed the treaties that ended the Thirty Years' War and the Eighty Years' War in a single, exhausted settlement. Four to eight million people had died. The Peace redrew central European borders, recognised Calvinism alongside Lutheranism and Catholicism, and, in the myth that later grew up around it, laid the foundations for the modern order of sovereign states.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The war that ended in 1648 had begun thirty years earlier as a Bohemian revolt. On 23 May 1618 Protestant nobles threw two Habsburg regents and their secretary out of a Prague castle window in the Second Defenestration; the men survived, allegedly saved by a dung heap, but the empire was plunged into confessional civil war. The Bohemians' offer of the crown to the Calvinist Elector Palatine Frederick V drew the whole of Europe into what began as an imperial and then became a religious and then a purely political conflict. Ferdinand II of Habsburg crushed the Bohemians at White Mountain in 1620, but by the 1620s the Danes under Christian IV had entered on the Protestant side, and by 1630 the Swedes under Gustavus Adolphus had landed on the Baltic coast at the head of a superb reformed army.

Gustavus Adolphus turned the war around at Breitenfeld in 1631 but died at Lützen in 1632; the Swedes were then hobbled at Nördlingen in 1634 and imperial victory again seemed possible. In 1635 Cardinal Richelieu brought Catholic France openly into the war against Catholic Habsburg Spain and Austria, purely to prevent the encirclement of France; from that point on the war became a struggle for European hegemony rather than religion. Battles blurred into blockades. Armies devoured the countryside; German towns were sacked and burnt (Magdeburg in 1631 was the most notorious), plague and typhus spread, and demographic collapse in Bohemia, Silesia, and much of Germany may have carried off a third to a half of the population.

By the early 1640s all sides were exhausted. Preliminary talks began at Hamburg in 1641; the imperial diet at Regensburg in 1641 to 1642 recognised that peace was necessary. The formal negotiations opened in December 1644 in the two Westphalian cities of Münster (chosen for the Catholic and imperial parties, since it was a Catholic prince-bishopric) and Osnabrück (chosen for the Protestant parties, since it alternated Catholic and Lutheran bishops). The two cities lay about fifty kilometres apart on the flat plain of northern Germany; couriers shuttled between them constantly. The papacy sent Fabio Chigi, the future Pope Alexander VII, as legate to Münster; the Venetian mediator Alvise Contarini worked alongside him.

The negotiations dragged on for almost four years while battles continued elsewhere. Ferdinand III, who had succeeded his father in 1637, was represented by Maximilian von Trauttmansdorff; France by the Duc de Longueville, Claude d'Avaux, and Abel Servien, under the general direction of Cardinal Mazarin in Paris; Sweden by Johan Oxenstierna, son of the great chancellor Axel, and Johan Adler Salvius. The Dutch United Provinces negotiated a separate peace with Spain, signed at Münster on 30 January 1648, formally recognising Dutch independence after eighty years of war. The two imperial treaties, of Münster (between the Emperor, Spain, and France) and Osnabrück (between the Emperor, Sweden, and the Protestant German estates), were finally signed on 24 October 1648.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The signing ceremony at Münster on the afternoon of 24 October 1648 was recorded, only weeks later, in the small, meticulous painting by the Dutch artist Gerard ter Borch that now hangs in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam. Delegates crowded round a table in the town hall; the Spanish and Dutch envoys, sworn enemies for eighty years, raised right hands to swear the treaty over a Bible and a reliquary. Ter Borch, present in Münster in some diplomatic capacity, inserted his own self-portrait on the far left. In Osnabrück on the same day, the imperial, Swedish, and Protestant German delegations signed their separate treaty. Together the two instruments, plus the Spanish-Dutch Peace of Münster of January 1648, constituted the Peace of Westphalia.

The territorial provisions redrew central Europe. France received sovereignty over the three Lorraine bishoprics of Metz, Toul, and Verdun (long occupied de facto), and the landgraviate of Upper and Lower Alsace, though the exact meaning of the Alsatian clauses was left ambiguous enough to fuel a century of border disputes. Sweden received Western Pomerania with the port of Stettin, the archbishoprics of Bremen and Verden, and the island of Wismar, giving her permanent bases on the Baltic and North Sea. Brandenburg-Prussia, an ambitious middle-ranking German state under the Great Elector Frederick William, received Farther Pomerania and four secularised bishoprics, laying the territorial foundation of the future Kingdom of Prussia. Bavaria kept the Upper Palatinate and the electoral dignity it had gained from the exiled Frederick V. The Swiss Confederation and the Dutch Republic were formally recognised as independent of the Empire.

The religious settlement built on the Peace of Augsburg of 1555. Catholicism, Lutheranism, and now Calvinism were all recognised as legal confessions of the Empire; the principle of cuius regio, eius religio (each ruler chooses the faith of his territory) was reaffirmed, but with a critical modification: the reference year for property held by each confession was set at 1 January 1624, freezing the ecclesiastical map at that pre-war date and preventing further seizures. Individual dissenters, at least in principle, were guaranteed the right of private worship and emigration. In practice, and especially in the Habsburg lands, Catholic re-conversion continued.

The constitutional impact on the Holy Roman Empire was profound. The three hundred or so member states, from powerful electorates to tiny imperial cities, were confirmed in their Landeshoheit or territorial supremacy: the right to raise armies, mint coins, tax, make treaties with foreign powers, and administer justice within their borders. The imperial diet remained, and the emperor could not tax, legislate, or declare war on the empire's behalf without its consent. In effect the Peace turned the Holy Roman Empire from a monarchy that had never quite worked into a federation of quasi-sovereign principalities under a mainly ceremonial imperial crown, an arrangement that lasted, more or less, until Napoleon dissolved it in 1806.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Ratification of the treaties dragged on into 1649 and 1650; the last imperial troops were only withdrawn from Germany in 1652. Rebuilding of shattered towns and depopulated villages took a generation. Bohemia and Moravia, ruthlessly re-Catholicised by the Habsburgs, remained under close Catholic control; Poland-Lithuania, unmentioned in the treaties, entered a decade of ruinous wars with Sweden, Russia, and its own Cossacks. Spain, defeated at Rocroi in 1643 and now saddled with recognising Dutch independence, continued its Franco-Spanish war until the Peace of the Pyrenees in 1659, ending the last chapter of a hundred years of Habsburg pre-eminence.

For France the Peace was a triumph of Richelieu's and Mazarin's diplomacy: the Habsburgs were surrounded, weakened, and prevented from ever again threatening French hegemony from both flanks. The young Louis XIV, ten years old in 1648, would exploit this position remorselessly in the wars that filled the second half of his long reign. For Sweden the Peace confirmed her as the great power of northern Europe and gave the crown a Baltic empire it would struggle to keep for another sixty years. For the Dutch Republic the January 1648 treaty at Münster was the definitive end of the Eighty Years' War and the beginning of the Dutch Golden Age of commerce, science, and painting.

The Papacy protested angrily. Pope Innocent X issued the bull Zelo Domus Dei, dated 26 November 1648 but formally published in 1650, declaring the Peace null and void because it acknowledged the legal existence of heretical confessions on Catholic soil. The protest was ignored. It was a marker of a shift in European affairs: for the first time in centuries, the great settlement of Christendom had been arranged without the Pope's substantive assent. The confessional age was passing, and the age of reason of state was arriving.

Within Germany the war and its ending shaped memory for centuries. Population losses in the worst-hit regions were not made up until the eighteenth century. Provincial identities hardened around the new territorial states; the idea of a unified German nation, when it eventually arose in the nineteenth century, would look back to Westphalia sometimes as a national humiliation and sometimes, in the more sober version of the historian Fritz Dickmann, as the great charter of German federalism.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The idea that the Peace of Westphalia founded the modern international order, sometimes called the Westphalian system, was largely a nineteenth- and twentieth-century construction. In its classic formulation, first articulated at length by the international lawyer Leo Gross in a 1948 article marking the treaty's three hundredth anniversary, Westphalia established the principles that states are sovereign within their own territories, that they are legally equal to one another regardless of size or religion, and that other states may not interfere in their internal affairs. These ideas were then said to have been carried into the modern law of nations by Vattel and codified in the Charter of the United Nations.

Recent scholarship, led by historians such as Andreas Osiander and Benno Teschke, has substantially punctured this myth. The treaties themselves do not use the language of sovereignty in the modern sense; the Holy Roman Empire, whose reform they principally concerned, was preserved rather than dissolved; the great powers continued to interfere routinely in one another's affairs. Even so, Westphalia does mark a real shift. After 1648 the Papacy no longer set the terms of European peace, religious confession no longer determined alliances (Louis XIV would happily support Muslim Ottomans and Protestant Swedes against Catholic Habsburgs), and the map of Europe was drawn primarily by considerations of dynastic and territorial balance.

Culturally Westphalia gave Europe a durable image of exhausted peace. The horrors of the Thirty Years' War, retold in Grimmelshausen's picaresque novel Simplicissimus of 1668 and reworked in Schiller's Wallenstein trilogy of 1799, provided a template for how modern Europe understood total war and its diplomatic settlements. The Peace's anniversaries have been reliably marked: the 350th in 1998 was celebrated with major exhibitions in both Münster and Osnabrück, and the twin cities still cultivate a shared memory as the Cradle of the Peace, a phrase inscribed on their joint tourist literature. Whatever the myths, the point remains: after 1648 Europe possessed a language and a set of practices for ending confessional war by treaty, and it would use them again and again.

worldContext.text: In the Andes the great silver mines of Potosí, at their late seventeenth-century peak, produced perhaps half of the world's silver, most of it flowing through Cartagena to Seville and thence into the European economy that had just been shattered by war. The English colonies on the Atlantic seaboard were expanding rapidly; the Bay Colony had established Harvard College in 1636, and Virginia had just imposed new tobacco duties. In Portuguese Brazil the eleven-year Dutch occupation of Recife and Pernambuco was in its last stages; it would end with the Portuguese recapture of Recife in 1654.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Ibrahim was in the middle of the long, ruinous Cretan War with Venice, which had begun in 1645; Ibrahim himself would be deposed and murdered in a Janissary coup in August 1648, weeks before Westphalia was signed, and succeeded by his six-year-old son Mehmed IV. In Safavid Persia Shah Abbas II ruled with the help of Grand Vizier Mohammad Beg. Cairo, under an Ottoman pasha, remained a great cosmopolitan entrepôt for pilgrims, coffee, and Indian Ocean trade.

In China the Ming dynasty had collapsed in April 1644 when Li Zicheng entered Beijing and the last Ming emperor hanged himself on Coal Hill; the Manchu Qing invaders had swept in the following month and proclaimed the young Shunzhi Emperor. In 1648 Qing forces under Prince Regent Dorgon were still consolidating control against Ming loyalist regimes in the south. In Japan the third Tokugawa shogun Iemitsu had just died in 1651, closing a country to almost all foreign contact except a Dutch trading post at Deshima and Chinese junks at Nagasaki.

The Mughal Empire under Shah Jahan was at its architectural zenith; the Taj Mahal at Agra had been essentially complete by 1643, and Shah Jahan had recently begun moving his capital to a new imperial city, Shahjahanabad (Old Delhi), inaugurated in 1648. In the Deccan, Mughal armies faced the emerging Maratha polity under the young Shivaji, who was starting the raids that would grow into an empire. Portuguese Goa was in slow decline; the Dutch had taken Malacca in 1641 and were pressing on the pepper coasts of Sri Lanka.

Songhai was gone, destroyed by a Moroccan invasion in 1591; its successor states in Timbuktu and along the Niger were fragmenting. In Ethiopia the emperor Fasilides had recently expelled the Portuguese Jesuits and moved his capital to Gondar, beginning a great period of Ethiopian architecture. The kingdom of Kongo was in political crisis; a Portuguese-Kongolese war would culminate in the disaster of Ambuila in 1665. On the Swahili coast Omani Arabs were beginning to challenge Portuguese control at Mombasa and Zanzibar.

The Dutch, from their base at Batavia, had emerged as the leading European maritime power in the region. Abel Tasman's voyages of 1642 to 1643 had reached Tasmania (which he named Van Diemen's Land), New Zealand, Tonga, and Fiji, but his encounters with Māori had been brief and violent, and the Dutch East India Company decided the southern lands were not worth pursuing commercially. Māori communities in Aotearoa continued their classic phase largely undisturbed; Polynesian and Melanesian societies across the wider Pacific were untouched by European contact.

figures.name: Emperor Ferdinand III
figures.role: Habsburg Holy Roman Emperor who authorised the concessions that ended the war

figures.name: Cardinal Jules Mazarin
figures.role: First minister of France directing the French negotiating team from Paris

figures.name: Maximilian von Trauttmansdorff
figures.role: Chief imperial negotiator at Münster and architect of the Habsburg concessions

figures.name: Johan Oxenstierna
figures.role: Leader of the Swedish delegation at Osnabrück, son of Chancellor Axel Oxenstierna

figures.name: Fabio Chigi
figures.role: Papal legate at Münster, later Pope Alexander VII, who condemned the treaty on behalf of Rome

images.caption: The Ratification of the Treaty of Münster, 15 May 1648, painted on copper by the Dutch master Gerard ter Borch, an eyewitness to the ceremony. Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam.
images.credit: Gerard ter Borch (1617 to 1681), via Wikimedia Commons
